one_line_takeaway: In this proof-of-concept LLM benchmark for C/C++ vulnerability detection, severity/access-complexity prediction, and repair, LoRA fine-tuning consistently beats zero-/few-shot prompting, code-specialized pretraining gives no reliable edge over general-purpose models (DeepSeek R1 beats DeepSeek-Coder-V2 everywhere), and standard code-similarity metrics (BLEU-4, CodeBLEU) disagree sharply with embedding-based ones (CodeBERTScore, ROUGE-L), casting doubt on their use as proxies for repair correctness.
key_finding: RQ1: Fine-tuning uniformly outperforms zero-shot and few-shot on Task 1 detection F1 across nearly all 10 models (e.g., DeepSeek R1 reaches 0.93 fine-tuned vs 0.70 zero-shot / 0.63 few-shot); few-shot performs no better than zero-shot despite the added examples. RQ2: Code-specific models do NOT consistently beat their general-purpose counterparts -- only 3 of 5 code-specific models show a marginal edge overall, and DeepSeek R1 (general) outperforms DeepSeek-Coder-V2 (code-specific) across every task and approach; in the few-shot setting code-specific models actually underperform on Task 1 (down ~18%) but outperform on Task 3 (up ~21%), showing task-dependence rather than a consistent code-specialization advantage. RQ3: Averaging F1 across tasks/approaches (Table 7), the Llama and DeepSeek model families are the most consistently strong pairs (e.g., DeepSeek pair 0.83/0.78/0.74 F1 across Tasks 1-3 fine-tuned); Mistral is strong individually but its pairing with the weaker Codestral drags down the duo ranking. RQ4: On repair generation (Table 8), fine-tuning again wins on every metric, with DeepSeek R1 topping CodeBERTScore (0.89), ROUGE-L (0.60), ChrF (0.42), and BLEU-4 (0.33), while the highest CodeBLEU (0.37) is achieved by Llama3.1 (in the few-shot setting). BLEU-4 and CodeBLEU stay low even when CodeBERTScore/ROUGE-L are high, which the authors interpret as evidence that current similarity metrics measure surface/token overlap rather than functional correctness of generated fixes.
core_contribution: An empirical benchmarking study (proof of concept for a Software Vulnerability Management workflow) that evaluates five pairs of open-source LLMs -- each pair consisting of a code-specialized model and its general-purpose counterpart (Llama3.1-8B/CodeLlama-7B, Gemma-7B/CodeGemma-7B, Qwen2.5-7B/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, Mistral-7B/Codestral-22B, DeepSeek R1-8B/DeepSeek-Coder-V2-6.7B) -- across a four-stage C/C++ vulnerability pipeline: (1) vulnerability detection, (2) severity prediction (CVSS-based Low/Medium/High), (3) access-complexity classification (High/Medium/Low), and (4) fix generation. Each task/model is run under three regimes -- LoRA fine-tuning (unsloth framework, 4-bit quantization, A100 GPU), zero-shot prompting, and few-shot prompting -- and the paper compares fine-tuning vs prompting, code-specific vs general-purpose models, best-performing model pairs, and the adequacy of standard code-similarity metrics for repair quality.
threat_model: This is a defensive evaluation/benchmarking paper, not an attack paper -- there is no adversary-vs-system threat model. The 'threats' under study are pre-existing software vulnerabilities in C/C++ code (CWE-classified weaknesses such as buffer overflows and SQL injection, CVE-linked entries with CVSS-derived severity and access-complexity labels), and the LLMs are evaluated purely as defensive automation for a detect -> assess-severity -> assess-access-complexity -> repair pipeline. No adversarial prompting, jailbreaking, or attacker capability model is considered.
limitations: Self-reported as 'Threats to Validity': (1) zero-/few-shot runs occasionally produced unparseable 'None' outputs, with inconsistent handling across models (Gemma noted as worse than Qwen/Mistral); (2) Big-Vul is known to contain mislabeled samples, which can distort accuracy estimates especially in zero-shot; (3) instruction-tuned models produced verbose outputs requiring larger max_new_tokens, longer inference, and extra post-processing/regex engineering; (4) experiments are limited to C-language repair and the specific classification tasks studied, so generalization to other languages/domains is unverified. The paper itself also flags that similarity-based repair metrics do not confirm functional correctness (models could produce non-matching but still-correct fixes, or matching-but-broken ones), and that reliance on a single reference fix per bug is a weak ground truth given multiple valid C repair rearrangements.

datasets_used: Big-Vul (348 GitHub projects, 91 vulnerability types, 2002-2019, 253,096 non-vulnerable / 11,823 vulnerable entries pre-preprocessing) -- used for Tasks 1-3 (detection, severity, access complexity)
VulRepair (8,482 vulnerable/repaired C function pairs from CVE-Fixes + Big-Vul, 1,754 projects, 1999-2021, BUGS/FIXS-tagged Huang et al. version) -- used for Task 4 (fix generation)
quantitative_results: Task 1 (detection) F1, fine-tuned: DeepSeek R1 0.93 (highest), Mistral-7B 0.85, Codestral-22B 0.79, CodeGemma-7B 0.76, CodeLlama-7B 0.75, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B 0.74, Gemma-7B 0.73, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 0.72, Llama3.1-8B 0.70, Qwen2.5-7B 0.67 (Table 4)
Task 1 F1, zero-shot best: CodeLlama-7B 0.76; few-shot best: CodeGemma-7B 0.79 (Table 4)
Fine-tune F1 across Tasks 1/2/3 for pairs (Table 7): Llama 0.72/0.78/0.78; Gemma 0.75/0.68/0.77; Qwen 0.70/0.78/0.76; Mistral 0.82/0.73/0.75; DeepSeek 0.83/0.78/0.74

Code-specific vs general F1 delta (Table 6), fine-tune Task1/2/3: CodeLlama +0.05/+0.01/+0.03; CodeGemma +0.03/+0.05/+0.04; Qwen2.5-Coder +0.07/+0.04/-0.01; Codestral -0.06/-0.21/-0.08; DeepSeek-Coder-V2 -0.21/-0.08/-0.01

Few-shot code-specific vs general delta: Task 1 drop of ~18% (code-specific worse), Task 3 gain of ~21% (code-specific better), per-model deltas in Table 6 (e.g., CodeLlama -0.08/+0.02/+0.21; DeepSeek-Coder-V2 -0.18/-0.07/+0.13)
Fix generation (Table 8), fine-tuned DeepSeek R1: CodeBERTScore 0.89, BLEU-4 0.33, CodeBLEU 0.34, ROUGE-L 0.60, ChrF 0.42 -- highest of all models/settings on CodeBERTScore, ROUGE-L, ChrF, BLEU-4
Highest CodeBLEU overall = 0.37, achieved by Llama3.1-8B in the few-shot setting (Table 8)
Fine-tuned Mistral-7B fix generation: CodeBERTScore 0.87, ROUGE-L 0.57, ChrF 0.36 (second-best overall per paper's discussion)
baselines_compared: Internal comparison only: fine-tuning vs zero-shot vs few-shot prompting (no external SOTA system used as a baseline)
Internal comparison: code-specialized model vs its general-purpose counterpart within each of 5 model pairs

Internal comparison: five reference-based text/code similarity metrics against each other (CodeBERTScore, CodeBLEU, ChrF, BLEU-4, ROUGE-L) to assess metric adequacy, not against an external evaluation baseline
